b: ^The name Alex Voss hasn't left your head in three days.
^Something you heard on the radio three nights ago hasn't left your head.

ep02_intro: ^The Voss Institute's public archive is housed in a repurposed municipal library on the edge of the old quarter. Reading rooms, microfiche machines, the particular silence of things that used to matter.
^The clerk at the front desk doesn't look up when you enter.
^Look for a reading room to work unobserved.
ep02_search_index: ^The public index is a wall of card-catalogue drawers — and, more usefully, three terminals running a digitised search interface from approximately 2003.
^You try variations. Project Halcyon. Operation Halcyon. Halcyon Protocol.
^On the fourth attempt — "Halcyon Trial" — the screen returns a single entry:

s: ^Halcyon / sub-file 7 / Meridian Health Group / restricted access — archivist authorisation required.*

ep02_ask_clerk: ^"I'm looking for materials related to a project called Halcyon. Possibly connected to the Meridian Health Group."
^The clerk looks up for the first time. Something behind her eyes recalibrates.
^"That section is restricted. You'd need a research authorisation code from the Institute itself."
^She has already looked back down. But her hand, you notice, is now resting on a desk phone.
ep02_press_clerk: ^"Who do I contact at the Institute to get that code?"
^The hand is definitely on the phone now.
ep02_find_quiet_room: ^Reading Room C is empty. A single high window throws a rectangle of pale winter light across a long table.
^You're about to sit when you notice someone already in the far corner — bent over a folder, making notes in a tight, controlled hand.
ep02_restricted_file: ^Sub-file 7. Restricted. You write the reference number in your notebook.
^Then — a sound from behind one of the reading room doors. Someone speaking quietly, urgently.
^You push the door open a few centimetres.
ep02_meet_morgan: ^"—the sequencing data doesn't match the published baseline. Not even close."

b: ^They're holding the same folder reference you just wrote down.

ep02_meet_morgan: ^Dark eyes. Cautious posture. The look of someone who has learned that interruptions are rarely coincidental.
^Sit down. "You were talking about sequencing data."
ep02_morgan_leave: ^You start to back out. Their voice stops you.
^"How did you get past the index? Sub-file 7 shouldn't surface in a public search."
ep02_morgan_halcyon_direct: ^"I'm looking for information on Halcyon. Whatever I can find."
ep02_morgan_eavesdrop: ^"You said the sequencing data doesn't match the published baseline."
^Their expression shifts — not quite trust, but the precursor to it.
ep02_morgan_conversation: ^"My name is Morgan. I'm a data analyst — or I was, until six months ago, when I was asked to stop asking questions about a particular clinical dataset."

b: ^"Does the name Alex Voss mean anything to you?"
^"Halcyon. Phase III trial data for the Meridian compound. Fourteen thousand patients."

ep02_morgan_reveal: ^"The published results show a 73% efficacy rate. The raw data — the data I saw before they locked me out — shows something very different."
^"Why are you still digging if they locked you out?"
ep02_morgan_numbers: ^"Thirty-one percent. And a cluster of adverse events in the 40-to-55 demographic that simply... disappeared from the final report."

b: ^"If Alex Voss really contacted you — they must have had access to the same files. Which means they're either inside Meridian or they were."

ep02_morgan_motive: ^"Because fourteen thousand people took that compound. Some of them are still taking it."
^"Someone should know the truth. I decided it would be me."
ep02_morgan_who: ^"A man named Carver. He's listed as lead auditor on the trial, but his credentials — I've checked — don't exist before 2019."
ep02_episode_end: ^The archive closes at five. You and Morgan leave together, neither of you quite ready to say the next thing out loud.
^"Meet me tomorrow. There's a copy I kept — offsite. If you're serious about this, you should see it."
^Tonight, for the first time in weeks, you don't feel entirely alone in this.
^That might be the most dangerous thing of all.
